title: Bound state in the continuum and dynamics via phase modulation in giant-atom waveguide setups
authors: Ji Qi, Xiaojun Zhang, Honngwei Yu, Zhihai Wang
abstract: Giant atoms, which couple to a waveguide through multiple spatially separated connection points beyond the dipole approximation, provide a versatile route for quantum information processing based on interference-induced bound states in the continuum (BICs). While multi-giant-atom architectures are being developed toward giant-atom quantum networks, the role of direct coupling between the giant atoms, in particular the associated coupling phase, in atomic dynamics remains insufficiently understood. Here we take a first step toward addressing this issue by studying a two-giant-atom waveguide-QED model. We show that the coupling phase can be used to control both the number of BICs and their profiles for both of photon and atoms. More interestingly, the presence of BICs gives rise to a variety of dynamical behaviors, providing an effective mechanism for tailoring quantum-state evolution in giant-atom waveguide-QED systems. Our results highlight coupling-phase engineering as a useful tool for controlling interference, bound states, and quantum dynamics in nonlocal light--matter interfaces.
summary: This paper explores how the phase of direct coupling between giant atoms can be used to engineer Bound States in the Continuum. By tuning this phase, one can control the number of BICs and the resulting quantum dynamics, ranging from complete decay to persistent population trapping. This offers a powerful tool for tailoring quantum-state evolution in waveguide-QED architectures.
main_problem: Investigating how the direct coupling phase between two giant atoms in a waveguide-QED setup can be used to control the formation of Bound States in the Continuum (BICs) and modulate quantum dynamics.
main_result: The study demonstrates that the coupling phase acts as a sensitive control parameter to tune the number of BICs (zero, one, or two) and their profiles, enabling tailored atomic dynamics such as population trapping, complete decay, or Rabi-like oscillations.
method: The authors use a non-Hermitian effective matrix approach derived via the Markov approximation and Fourier transformation to analyze the eigenvalues and time-dependent atomic amplitudes.
model_or_system: A two-braided-giant-atom waveguide-QED model consisting of two directly coupled two-level atoms coupled to a one-dimensional coupled-resonator waveguide (CRW) at multiple spatially separated sites.
key_observables: Imaginary parts of the system eigenvalues (to identify BICs), atomic excitation amplitudes, photonic distribution across the waveguide, and atomic entanglement (concurrence).
important_parameters: Coupling phase (phi), atomic size (N), relative separation (Delta), and photon hopping strength (xi).
main_assumptions: Rotating-wave approximation (RWA), Markov approximation, and the thermodynamic limit for the resonator array.
figures_summary: Figure 3 shows time evolution of atomic populations (trapping, decay, and oscillations); Figure 4 and 5 show spatial profiles of BICs and atomic state tomography for different configurations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the giant-atom model, presents the mathematical framework for the non-Hermitian dynamics, analyzes the dependence of BICs on the coupling phase and geometry, and concludes with the implications for quantum state engineering.
why_interesting: It provides a versatile framework for engineering dissipation-free states and controlling interference in non-local light-matter interfaces, which is highly relevant for designing quantum networks and state storage in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
